title: Giant Snowflakes Fall from the Sky
story: Imagine snowflakes as big as dinner plates! In 1887, during a fierce snowstorm at Fort Keogh, Montana, people witnessed an incredible sight on January 28. Enormous snowflakes drifted down, reportedly measuring a whopping 15 inches wide and 8 inches thick. These fluffy giants were so large that catching one would be like holding a small, icy pillow in your hands. It's a frosty record that still amazes us today!
activity_question: How wide were the largest snowflakes reported to be?
follow_up_question: How do you think these giant snowflakes would feel if you caught one?

title: Bee-zarre Survival: A Sting-credible Tale
story: Buzz off, danger! In 1962, Johanne Relleke faced a swarm of angry bees in Rhodesia. Incredibly, she survived an astounding 2,443 stings! title: Monkey's Space Adventure in Iranian Rocket
story: A brave little monkey blasted off into the stars! On January 28, 2013, Iran's Pishgam rocket zoomed into space with a furry passenger on board. This mission helped Iran test important space technology and learn more about keeping living creatures safe beyond our planet.

title: Lost Bird Found: The Cahow's Comeback
story: Swooping through the air, a mysterious bird caught the eye of naturalists in 1951. On January 28, an American and Bermudan team made an incredible discovery on remote rocky crags off Bermuda. They found the nesting site of the Bermuda petrel, also called the cahow. This sea bird was thought extinct since 1615! Sporadic sightings had sparked hope, leading to this exciting expedition that proved the cahow still existed.

title: Moon Selfie: Earth's First Lunar Photo
story: Smile, Moon! In 1960, scientists made space history by taking Earth's first lunar selfie. On January 28, they beamed a radar signal from Washington, D.C. all the way to the Moon. Like a cosmic game of catch, the signal bounced back, carrying an image of our lunar neighbor. This groundbreaking photograph marked a giant leap in space exploration, showing we could send and receive information across vast distances.

bonus_fact: This lunar photo paved the way for future space communication and imaging techniques.

title: A Railway Connects Two Oceans
story: Choo-choo! A train whistle echoed across Panama in 1855. For the first time ever, a locomotive chugged its way from the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean on January 28. The Panama Railway made history as the first track to span an entire continent. This amazing feat connected two vast bodies of water, making travel and trade much easier.
activity_question: What did the Panama Railway connect?
activity_choices: The Atlantic Ocean and the Pacific Ocean
activity_answer: The Atlantic Ocean and the Pacific Ocean
bonus_fact: The Panama Railway is older than the more famous Panama Canal.

title: Ancient Plague Mystery Solved
story: Scientists cracked a centuries-old mystery on January 28, 2014. Using advanced DNA analysis, they discovered that the Plague of Justinian from the 6th century was caused by the same type of bacteria as the Black Death. This ancient illness, which affected millions, was a variant of Yersinia pestis. The finding helps us understand how diseases change over time.
activity_question: What did scientists discover about the Plague of Justinian?
activity_choices: It was caused by the same type of bacteria as the Black Death
It was less deadly than previously thought
It originated in a different country
It was actually a viral infection
activity_answer: It was caused by the same type of bacteria as the Black Death
follow_up_question: Why is it important to study ancient diseases?

title: Manitoba Women Break Voting Barriers
story: Cheers erupted across Manitoba! Women there won a hard-fought battle for equality. In 1916, on January 28, they became the first in Canada allowed to vote in provincial elections. But the victory didn't stop there. These trailblazing women also gained the right to run for office themselves. It was a huge step forward, opening doors for women's voices to be heard in government.
activity_question: What two rights did Manitoba women win in 1916?
activity_choices: The right to vote and hold office
The right to drive and own property
The right to work and attend university
The right to fly planes and join the army
activity_answer: The right to vote and hold office
follow_up_question: Why do you think it was important for women to have the right to vote?

title: Soviets Shock US in Basketball Upset
story: Squeaking sneakers and gasps filled the air as the Soviet Union's basketball team achieved the unthinkable. They defeated the United States 62-37 in 1959, handing the Americans their first-ever loss in international play. story: Brrr! Teeth chattering and icicles forming, the people of Cynthiana, Kentucky, shivered through the coldest day in state history. On January 28, 1963, temperatures plummeted to an unbelievable -34°F (-37°C). Imagine frozen breath and frosty windows as families huddled indoors, sipping hot cocoa and bundling up in every blanket they could find. This arctic blast set a chilling record that still stands today!
activity_question: What was the record-breaking temperature in Cynthiana, Kentucky?
follow_up_question: How do you think people stayed warm during this extreme cold?

title: Brrr! New Hampshire's Chilliest Day
story: Teeth chattering, icicles forming on eyelashes! In 1925, Pittsburgh, New Hampshire experienced its coldest temperature ever. On January 28, the mercury plummeted to a bone-chilling -46°F (-43°C). That's colder than your freezer! Brave residents bundled up in layers, sipped hot cocoa, and huddled by fireplaces to stay warm.
activity_question: What was the record-breaking temperature in Pittsburgh, New Hampshire?
follow_up_question: How do you think people stayed warm during such extreme cold?

title: Chandler Harper's Golfing Glory
story: Swinging into victory, Chandler Harper claimed the top spot at the 29th PGA Seniors Golf Championship in 1968. On January 28 that year, this skilled golfer outplayed his competitors on the green, showcasing precision and experience. The PGA Seniors tournament brings together golf's most seasoned players, proving that age is just a number when it comes to mastering the game.
activity_question: What event did Chandler Harper win in 1968?
follow_up_question: Why do you think golf is a sport that people can play well even as they get older?

title: Baseball Legends Join the Hall of Fame
story: Three baseball greats stepped into the spotlight in 1973. On January 28, Mickey Welch, George Kelly, and Billy Evans were elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame. These talented players and officials left their mark on America's favorite pastime. Their skills on the field and contributions to the game earned them a place among baseball's most honored heroes.
activity_question: Who was elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame in 1973?
activity_choices: Mickey Welch, George Kelly, and Billy Evans
Babe Ruth, Lou Gehrig, and Ty Cobb
Jackie Robinson, Willie Mays, and Hank Aaron
Yogi Berra, Mickey Mantle, and Joe DiMaggio
activity_answer: Mickey Welch, George Kelly, and Billy Evans
follow_up_question: What do you think a player needs to do to be elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame?

title: Powering the Future: A Nuclear Milestone
story: Atoms split and energy surged as a groundbreaking project began on January 28, 1958. Scientists and engineers kicked off construction on the first private thorium-uranium nuclear reactor. This wasn't your average power plant! By combining thorium and uranium, they hoped to create a safer, more efficient way to generate electricity. It marked a bold step into the atomic age, sparking dreams of a world powered by the tiniest particles.
activity_question: What two elements were combined in this special nuclear reactor?
follow_up_question: Why might scientists want to create safer nuclear reactors?
